rescue_reasoning: The source explicitly supports a transmission expansion aimed at grid congestion and reliability: "Build the 150 kV ... power line to reduce congestion and improve safety and quality of electricity transmission service." Although it lacks an explicit emissions/renewables token (gate failure), the provided climate_screen is "explicit_self", which breaks the tie in favor of keeping the row as climate-relevant. The existing primary category (Mitigation) is consistent with the canonical mechanism (grid expansion to relieve congestion/reliability), so no reclassification is needed.
policy_statement: Build the 150 kV SE S. Teresa–Buddusò power line to reduce congestion and improve safety and quality of electricity transmission service.
mechanism_description: Builds a new power transmission line to reduce grid congestion and improve the safety and quality (reliability) of electricity transmission service.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid infrastructure investment → ↓ congestion/curtailment + ↑ ability to transmit low-carbon power → ↓ fossil generation/redispatch → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the electricity system by adding/strengthening high-voltage transmission capacity to relieve bottlenecks and improve operational reliability. (2) This reduces line congestion and losses, lowers the need for fossil-based redispatch and reserve operation, and increases the ability to connect and deliver variable renewables from resource-rich areas to load centers (reducing renewable curtailment). (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower grid-average emissions intensity and total CO₂e from electricity generation (with reliability as a co-benefit).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling greater delivery and utilization of low-carbon electricity (and less curtailment/inefficient redispatch), thereby reducing power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: expanding transmission reduces congestion and enables cleaner dispatch/greater renewable delivery, which cuts fossil generation and associated GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: while improved reliability can support adaptation/resilience outcomes, the most direct and typically largest climate impact of congestion relief transmission is emissions reduction through generation mix and dispatch changes. It is not primarily “Resource Efficiency” because the key benefit is decarbonizing supplied electricity rather than mainly reducing energy input for the same service (loss reductions are secondary), and it is not “Nature-Based Solutions.”  
3) Edge cases applied: if a specific project is built mainly for hazard hardening (e.g., wildfire risk reduction, storm resilience) with no material change in generation mix, it could lean Adaptation; however the stated mechanism here is congestion reduction/reliability of transmission service, which commonly functions as an enabler of low-carbon integration and thus mitigation-dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances of this mechanism: any policy whose core mechanism is transmission expansion to relieve congestion/reliability constraints operates through grid operational changes that can reduce fossil dispatch and/or enable renewables, making mitigation the appropriate primary category for the mechanism family.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: any secondary subtype (e.g., “enabling renewables integration” vs. “reducing losses” vs. “resilience co-benefit”) must be determined at the individual policy level based on project context and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the line is justified and operated primarily for resilience/safety (e.g., undergrounding to reduce fire risk) without affecting generation dispatch or renewable integration, the dominant climate effect could shift toward Adaptation; conversely, when built to unlock renewable zones or relieve fossil-heavy constrained areas, mitigation impacts are stronger.
key_indicators: "Build the 150 kV ... power line"; "reduce congestion"; "improve the safety and quality of the electricity transmission service"
co_benefits: Improved grid reliability/service quality; improved safety
instance_edge_case_notes: Emissions reductions are not explicitly stated; climate impact is inferred via congestion relief and improved transmission enabling lower-carbon dispatch/integration.
